problem_statement.Title: pylint --notes ignores punctuation-only note tags (W0511 not reported for tags like "???")
problem_statement.Description: When a note tag passed via --notes consists entirely of punctuation (no word characters), pylint's fixme detection does not report the W0511 warning for that tag. The problem appears in the misc checker that builds and applies the note-matching regex.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect boundary anchor used when building the fixme/notes regex — location: pylint/checkers/misc.py::EncodingChecker.open(). The code appends a word-boundary anchor ("\b") to the pattern (e.g. regex_string = rf"#\s*({notes}|{self.config.notes_rgx})\b"). This encodes the assumption that a word-boundary correctly prevents matching inside a larger word while allowing any legal trailing character. That assumption is false for tags that end with non-word characters.

2) Interaction with escaped punctuation tags — location: pylint/checkers/misc.py::EncodingChecker.open(). User-supplied tags are escaped with re.escape (so a tag like "???" becomes "\?\?\?" in the alternation). Because "\b" only matches between a word-character (\w) and a non-word-character, a punctuation-only tag (all non-word chars) followed by a non-word char (e.g. ':' or whitespace or end-of-line) will not satisfy "\b", so the tag never matches.

3) Matching approach that exposes the boundary problem — location: pylint/checkers/misc.py where comments are matched by searching the compiled pattern against "#" + comment_text.lower() with the regex compiled re.I. This confirms the pattern's trailing boundary is the deciding factor for whether a match occurs; the use of case-folding/flags does not mitigate the boundary mismatch for punctuation-only tags.

4) Correct diagnostic relationship for the hardcoded boundary (diagnosis, not fix prescription): the current hardcoded boundary value is "\\b". The intended requirement is: "the character after the tag must NOT be a word character". Expressed as a regex boundary, that relationship should be a negative lookahead such as "(?!\\w)" (equivalently "(?=\\W|$)"). Concretely, the regex string should end with {boundary} where boundary = "(?!\\w)", e.g. rf"#\s*({notes}|{self.config.notes_rgx}){boundary}" — this preserves the intent (don't match inside a larger word) while allowing punctuation-only tags to be matched.
StepsToReproduce: Create a file named test.py with the following contents:
Run pylint specifying both tags (word and punctuation) with: pylint test.py --notes="YES,???"
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior:
Pylint should emit a W0511 (fixme) warning for both comment lines. Example output should include both:
************* Module test
test.py:1:1: W0511: YES: yes (fixme)
test.py:2:1: W0511: ???: no (fixme)
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior:
Pylint emits a W0511 for the first (word) tag but not for the punctuation-only tag. Example output shows only:
************* Module test
test.py:1:1: W0511: YES: yes (fixme)
(the line with '???: no' produces no W0511)

hints_text: Did a little investigation, this is we're actually converting this option in a regular expression pattern (thereby making it awfully similar to the `notes-rgx` option). Since `?` is a special character in regex this doesn't get picked up. Using `\?\?\?` in either `notes` or `notes-rgx` should work.

problem_statement.Title: Traceback printed for unrecognized option instead of a clean error/exit
problem_statement.Description: When an unrecognized command-line option is passed to pylint (for example `pylint -Q`), pylint prints the reporter message about the unrecognized option but then raises pylint.config.exceptions._UnrecognizedOptionError which is not caught on the CLI path, producing a Python traceback to stderr.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) _config_initialization (pylint/config/config_initialization.py) detects unrecognized command-line options, calls linter.add_message("unrecognized-option", ...), and then raises pylint.config.exceptions._UnrecognizedOptionError(options=...).
2) Run.__init__ (pylint/lint/run.py) calls _config_initialization but does not catch _UnrecognizedOptionError. Because the exception is uncaught on the CLI path, Python prints a full traceback even though the reporter message has already been emitted.
3) The CLI orchestration code follows the pattern of printing a friendly message and exiting (e.g., ArgumentPreprocessingError and config-file OSError are handled with printed messages and sys.exit(32)). The absence of a matching except _UnrecognizedOptionError handler in Run.__init__ breaks that UX expectation. 

Evidence/preservation of values/formats: the code path already produces a reporter message like
"Command line:1:0: E0015: Unrecognized option found: Q (unrecognized-option)" (this message is emitted immediately by the reporter when linter.add_message is called). The defect is that the subsequent raise is not handled and therefore a traceback appears instead of a clean exit (the correct behavior is to exit non-zero without a traceback, consistent with other early-exit error handling which uses sys.exit(32)).
StepsToReproduce: Run pylint with an unrecognized short option, e.g. `pylint -Q`
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Pylint prints a concise, user-facing error message about the unrecognized option (for example the existing reporter message) and exits with a non-zero status (consistent with other early argument/configuration errors). No Python traceback should be printed to stderr.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Pylint prints the reporter message about the unrecognized option and then a full Python traceback is printed to stderr showing pylint.config.exceptions._UnrecognizedOptionError (stack trace bubbles out of Run.__init__).

problem_statement.Title: --recursive=y ignores ignore-paths from pyproject.toml (pylint)
problem_statement.Description: When running pylint with --recursive=y, ignore-paths configured in pyproject.toml are not applied and files under ignored paths (e.g. src/gen/*) are still linted.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Inconsistent option source in PyLinter: pylint/lint/pylinter.py::_expand_files calls expand_modules(..., self._ignore_paths) instead of using the active configuration attribute. expand_modules expects the ignore-paths argument to come from the current configuration (a list of compiled regex patterns).  

2) Stale, duplicated attribute created in PyLinter.open(): pylint/lint/pylinter.py::open assigns self._ignore_paths = self.linter.config.ignore_paths. This creates a separate/statically-initialized attribute (self._ignore_paths) that may be out-of-sync with the authoritative option container self.config (which can change, e.g. with per-directory namespaces). Because _expand_files uses that stale attribute, ignore-paths loaded from pyproject.toml (self.config.ignore_paths) may not be applied during module expansion.  

3) The correct relationship that must hold (and is not being honored) is: expand_modules(modules, self.config.ignore, self.config.ignore_patterns, self.config.ignore_paths). Currently the call uses self._ignore_paths (wrong). Also, open() currently performs: self._ignore_paths = self.linter.config.ignore_paths (wrong duplication); the effective/authoritative source of option values should be self.config.ignore_paths.
StepsToReproduce: Run from the project root: pylint --recursive=y src/
Observe that files under src/gen/ are still reported by pylint (e.g. src/gen/about.py:... messages).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When running pylint with --recursive=y and ignore-paths configured in pyproject.toml, files whose paths match ignore-paths (for example src/gen/*) are excluded from linting and do not appear in pylint output.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Pylint reports issues from files under ignored paths (for example src/gen/about.py, src/gen/design.py, etc.) even though ignore-paths is set in pyproject.toml.

## Fails for some reason

EDIT: Just upgraded to 2.14.3, and still seems to report the same.
Hmm I can reproduce your error, and now I understand the root cause. The root cause is following. The decision of skipping the path is here:

https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/blob/3c5eca2ded3dd2b59ebaf23eb289453b5d2930f0/pylint/lint/pylinter.py#L600-L607

* When you execute pylint with `src/` argument following variables are present:
```python
(Pdb) p root
'src/gen'
(Pdb) p self.config.ignore_paths
[re.compile('^src\\\\gen\\\\.*$|^src/gen/.*$')]
```

* When you uexecute pylint with `.` argument following variables are present:
```python
(Pdb) p root
'./src/gen'
(Pdb) p self.config.ignore_paths
[re.compile('^src\\\\gen\\\\.*$|^src/gen/.*$')]
```

In the second case, the source is prefixed with `./` which causes that path is not matched. The simple fix should be to use  `os.path.normpath()` https://docs.python.org/3/library/os.path.html#os.path.normpath

patch:
diff --git a/pylint/lint/expand_modules.py b/pylint/lint/expand_modules.py
--- a/pylint/lint/expand_modules.py
+++ b/pylint/lint/expand_modules.py
@@ -82,8 +82,10 @@ def expand_modules(
             continue
         module_path = get_python_path(something)
         additional_search_path = [".", module_path] + path
-        if os.path.exists(something):
-            # this is a file or a directory
+        if os.path.isfile(something) or os.path.exists(
+            os.path.join(something, "__init__.py")
+        ):
+            # this is a file or a directory with an explicit __init__.py
             try:
                 modname = ".".join(
                     modutils.modpath_from_file(something, path=additional_search_path)
@@ -103,9 +105,7 @@ def expand_modules(
                 )
                 if filepath is None:
                     continue
-            except (ImportError, SyntaxError) as ex:
-                # The SyntaxError is a Python bug and should be
-                # removed once we move away from imp.find_module: https://bugs.python.org/issue10588
+            except ImportError as ex:
                 errors.append({"key": "fatal", "mod": modname, "ex": ex})
                 continue
         filepath = os.path.normpath(filepath)

problem_statement.Title: pylint fails when a directory contains a module with the same name (false __init__.py assumption)
problem_statement.Description: When a package-like directory (e.g. 'a') contains a module with the same name (a/a.py) but no __init__.py, running `pylint a` produces a parse error complaining about a/__init__.py missing. The linter attempts to parse a non-existent a/__init__.py and fails.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1) expand_modules assumes a directory argument always corresponds to a package file and unconditionally constructs filepath = os.path.join(something, "__init__.py"). Location: pylint/lint/expand_modules.py (expand_modules). This encodes the incorrect assumption that a directory necessarily has an __init__.py. 2) expand_modules unconditionally appends a top-level module descriptor for that filepath when the module is not a namespace, even if filepath does not exist. Location: pylint/lint/expand_modules.py (the code path that builds and appends the module descriptor). This causes a non-existent a/__init__.py to be added to the module list and later parsed (triggering F0010). 3) expand_modules uses os.path.dirname(filepath) as the directory to enumerate submodules (modutils.get_module_files(os.path.dirname(filepath), ...)). That is correct only when filepath is a real __init__.py (so dirname(filepath) == package dir). When filepath is set to the directory (or when __init__.py does not exist), using os.path.dirname(filepath) will point to the parent directory instead of the package directory. Location: pylint/lint/expand_modules.py (the code that calls modutils.get_module_files). Correct relationships implied by the investigation (diagnostic facts preserved): - Only treat a directory as a package if os.path.exists(os.path.join(dir, "__init__.py")) is True. - A top-level module descriptor should only be created when the referenced file actually exists (os.path.isfile(filepath) == True). - The directory passed to get_module_files must be the package directory itself; equivalently: dir_to_list = os.path.dirname(filepath) if os.path.basename(filepath) == "__init__.py" else filepath.
StepsToReproduce: Create directory structure: ./a/a.py and ./a/b.py (both files empty), without a/__init__.py.
Observe the parse error about a/__init__.py.
Optional: mv a/a.py a/c.py then run pylint a to see it succeed.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Running `pylint a` where 'a' is a directory without an __init__.py should succeed (pylint should lint the Python files inside the directory). No parse error should be reported for a non-existent a/__init__.py.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: pylint reports: F0010 error while code parsing: Unable to load file a/__init__.py: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: 'a/__init__.py' and fails. This can also cause secondary module-resolution errors when linting multiple targets (for example, running `pylint -E r a` can produce spurious import/no-name errors because a bogus a/__init__.py entry was added).

diff --git a/pylint/config/argument.py b/pylint/config/argument.py
--- a/pylint/config/argument.py
+++ b/pylint/config/argument.py
@@ -99,11 +99,20 @@ def _py_version_transformer(value: str) -> tuple[int, ...]:
     return version
 
 
+def _regex_transformer(value: str) -> Pattern[str]:
+    """Return `re.compile(value)`."""
+    try:
+        return re.compile(value)
+    except re.error as e:
+        msg = f"Error in provided regular expression: {value} beginning at index {e.pos}: {e.msg}"
+        raise argparse.ArgumentTypeError(msg)
+
+
 def _regexp_csv_transfomer(value: str) -> Sequence[Pattern[str]]:
     """Transforms a comma separated list of regular expressions."""
     patterns: list[Pattern[str]] = []
     for pattern in _csv_transformer(value):
-        patterns.append(re.compile(pattern))
+        patterns.append(_regex_transformer(pattern))
     return patterns
 
 
@@ -130,7 +139,7 @@ def _regexp_paths_csv_transfomer(value: str) -> Sequence[Pattern[str]]:
     "non_empty_string": _non_empty_string_transformer,
     "path": _path_transformer,
     "py_version": _py_version_transformer,
-    "regexp": re.compile,
+    "regexp": _regex_transformer,
     "regexp_csv": _regexp_csv_transfomer,
     "regexp_paths_csv": _regexp_paths_csv_transfomer,
     "string": pylint_utils._unquote,

problem_statement.Title: pylintrc regexp with \p{...} (e.g. \p{Han}) causes unhandled re.error during config parsing
problem_statement.Description: When a .pylintrc option of type 'regexp' contains an escape sequence not supported by Python's builtin re (for example '\p{Han}'), pylint crashes with an unhandled re.error while parsing the configuration.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1) pylint/config/argument.py: _TYPE_TRANSFORMERS contains the entry "regexp": re.compile. Incorrect assumption: calling re.compile directly as an argparse type function is safe. Reality: re.compile can raise re.error for patterns unsupported by Python's re (e.g. '\p{Han}'); that exception is allowed to propagate out of argparse and becomes an uncaught crash. The transformer should convert re.error into argparse.ArgumentTypeError (i.e. the type-transformer must catch re.error and surface a controlled argparse error). 2) pylint/config/argument.py: _regexp_csv_transfomer calls re.compile on each CSV token and does not catch re.error. Incorrect assumption: CSV transformers will not receive patterns that fail to compile. Correct relationship: each compiled pattern can raise re.error and the transformer must catch that and raise an argparse.ArgumentTypeError for that option. 3) pylint/config/argument.py: _regexp_paths_csv_transfomer builds path-based patterns and calls re.compile without catching re.error. Incorrect assumption: path-regex compilation cannot fail. Correct relationship: pattern compilation may raise re.error and the transformer must catch it and raise argparse.ArgumentTypeError. 4) pylint/config/option.py: VALIDATORS['regexp'] = lambda pattern: re.compile(pattern or "") (optparse path). Note: the optparse validation path already wraps validator exceptions into optparse.OptionValueError via _call_validator, so the optparse path does not produce the same uncaught crash. The crash in user report is specific to the argparse transformer path because argparse type functions (currently set to re.compile) let re.error bubble up.
StepsToReproduce: Create a .pylintrc containing a regexp option such as:
Run: pylint (in the same project / environment)
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Pylint should not terminate with an unhandled Python exception when parsing .pylintrc containing '\p{...}' escapes. At config-parse time it should present a clear, non-crashing configuration error indicating the regular expression is invalid for Python's re engine (or otherwise accept the pattern if the engine supports it).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Pylint crashes with an uncaught exception during configuration parsing. The process raises re.error: "bad escape \p at position 1" (stack trace shows re.compile called from argparse type conversion while parsing the pylintrc), causing pylint to abort with a Python stack trace.

hints_text:
This doesn't seem like it is a `pylint` issue?

`re.compile("[\p{Han}a-z_]")` also raises normally. `\p` also isn't documented: https://docs.python.org/3/howto/regex.html
Is this a supported character?
I think this could be improved! Similar to the helpful output when passing an unrecognized option to Pylint, we could give a friendly output indicating that the regex pattern is invalid without the traceback; happy to put a MR together if you agree.
Thanks @mbyrnepr2 I did not even realize it was a crash that we had to fix before your comment.
@mbyrnepr2 I think in the above stacktrace on line 1858 makes the most sense.

We need to decide though if we continue to run the program. I think it makes sense to still quit. If we catch regex errors there and pass we will also "allow" ignore path regexes that don't work. I don't think we should do that.

Imo, incorrect regexes are a little different from other "incorrect" options, since there is little risk that they are valid on other interpreters or versions such as old messages etc. Therefore, I'd prefer to (cleanly) exit.
Indeed @DanielNoord I think we are on the same page regarding this point; I would also exit instead of passing if the regex is invalid. That line you mention, we can basically try/except on re.error and exit printing the details of the pattern which is invalid.

patch:
diff --git a/pylint/reporters/text.py b/pylint/reporters/text.py
--- a/pylint/reporters/text.py
+++ b/pylint/reporters/text.py
@@ -175,7 +175,7 @@ def on_set_current_module(self, module: str, filepath: str | None) -> None:
         self._template = template
 
         # Check to see if all parameters in the template are attributes of the Message
-        arguments = re.findall(r"\{(.+?)(:.*)?\}", template)
+        arguments = re.findall(r"\{(\w+?)(:.*)?\}", template)
         for argument in arguments:
             if argument[0] not in MESSAGE_FIELDS:
                 warnings.warn(

diff --git a/tests/reporters/unittest_reporting.py b/tests/reporters/unittest_reporting.py

 from pylint import checkers

         in records[1].message.args[0]
@@ -113,7 +110,24 @@ def test_template_option_non_existing(linter) -> None:
     assert out_lines[2] == "my_mod:2::()"
 
 
-def test_deprecation_set_output(recwarn):
+def test_template_option_with_header(linter: PyLinter) -> None:

+    linter.open()

+    linter.add_message("C0301", line=1, args=(1, 2))

+

+
+
+def test_deprecation_set_output(recwarn: WarningsRecorder) -> None:
     """TODO remove in 3.0."""
     reporter = BaseReporter()
     # noinspection PyDeprecation

problem_statement.Title: msg-template with literal braces (double braces) triggers incorrect warning and breaks output
problem_statement.Description: When a msg-template that uses escaped/literal braces (the '{{' '}}' form) is passed, Pylint emits a spurious "Don't recognize the argument ..." warning and the formatted messages are corrupted (only fragments like '" }' are printed) instead of the intended literal-brace output.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) The text reporter uses a regex to parse format fields in pylint/reporters/text.py -> TextReporter.on_set_current_module: re.findall(r"\{(.+?)(:.*)?\}", template). This regex does not understand Python's format-string escaping for literal braces (double '{{' and '}}') or nested placeholders, so it can capture fragments such as ' "Category"' (including quotes/spaces) as an argument name and treat literal-brace content as a field.

2) The code then removes unrecognized placeholders using re.sub(r"\{" + argument[0] + r"(:.*?)?\}", "", template). Because argument[0] can contain spaces, quotes or nested-brace text, this substitution removes parts of the user's template that were intended to be literal text (the braces and surrounding text), producing the broken output seen.

3) The warning message and the decision to remove text are based on the raw regex capture (the incorrectly parsed argument string). Location of the behavior: pylint/reporters/text.py, class TextReporter, method on_set_current_module. Exact patterns involved: find pattern r"\{(.+?)(:.*)?\}" and removal pattern r"\{" + argument[0] + r"(:.*?)?\}". The correct parsing relationship required is: format placeholders and escaped braces must be interpreted according to Python's format-string rules (double '{{'/'}}' represent literal braces) so only real field names (e.g., 'category') are treated as placeholders.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Each lint message prints the dictionary-like string with literal braces and the Category key, for example: { "Category": "convention" }, and no warning about an unrecognized argument is emitted.

hints_text:
Subsequently, there is also this behavior with the quotes
```
$ pylint test.py --msg-template='"Category": "{category}"'
************* Module test
Category": "convention
Category": "error
Category": "error
Category": "convention
Category": "convention
Category": "error

$ pylint test.py --msg-template='""Category": "{category}""'
************* Module test
"Category": "convention"
"Category": "error"
"Category": "error"
"Category": "convention"
"Category": "convention"
"Category": "error"
```
Commit that changed the behavior was probably this one: https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/commit/7c3533ca48e69394391945de1563ef7f639cd27d#diff-76025f0bc82e83cb406321006fbca12c61a10821834a3164620fc17c978f9b7e

And I tested on 2.11.1 that it is working as intended on that version.
Thanks for digging into this !
